structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective EHR data from a large hospital system or network spanning 36 months of ICU and ED encounters. All timestamped vital signs (HR, BP, RR, temp, O2 sat), lab results (lactate, WBC, CRP, blood cultures), medication administrations, and clinician-entered diagnoses and orders. Minimum 50,000 patient encounters with sepsis labels derivable from clinical adjudication (e.g., qSOFA criteria onset, blood culture + antibiotic initiation within a 24-hour window, or validated sepsis trigger algorithms applied consistently). Data quality: known missingness patterns (e.g., less frequent monitoring in stable patients, batch lab orders) are preserved; no imputation of missing values prior to feature engineering. Sites within the network are identified and stratified in analysis.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: sepsis onset within 6 hours of the observation window close. The observation window is anchored at a reference time T; sepsis onset is defined as the earliest of (a) charted sepsis diagnosis with supporting clinical criteria (systemic infection + ≥2 SIRS criteria or qSOFA ≥ 2), or (b) initiation of broad-spectrum antibiotics preceded by blood culture order within 6 hours, or (c) clinical adjudication by trained reviewers blind to model predictions. All labels are assigned retrospectively at discharge. To avoid label-leakage and clinical delay bias: labels are locked at the end of the 6-hour window; any diagnosis or antibiotic order within that window is included; any order placed after the window closes is excluded. This ensures the model predicts true future onset, not already-declared sepsis.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with site stratification. Months 1–24 (first 24 months) are training; months 25–30 (next 6 months) are validation; months 31–36 (final 6 months) are held-out test. Within each temporal fold, patient encounters are grouped by clinical site (if multicenter data); no single patient appears in multiple splits. This temporal split is justified because (1) it prevents leakage of future information into model development, (2) it tests generalization under changing clinical practice, documentation, and sepsis epidemiology, and (3) it mirrors the realistic deployment scenario where the model is built on historical data and evaluated on prospective performance. The 24-month training window provides sufficient labeled examples (expected ~8,000–15,000 sepsis cases) while allowing the validation and test sets to represent more recent practice. Site stratification allows detection of site-specific confounds (e.g., different documentation practices, sepsis detection thresholds).
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation models, encoding schemes) are fit only on the training set (months 1–24). The fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Specifically: (a) for continuous vitals and labs, z-score normalization is computed on the training set's non-missing values; (b) for categorical variables (e.g., admission source), one-hot encodings are defined on training set categories; (c) missing value indicators (binary flags for missingness) are computed during feature engineering and require no fitting. Any temporal feature (e.g., time since last measurement) is computed per-encounter using the reference time T, so no global fitting is needed. This ensures the validation and test sets are treated as truly held-out data and prevents data leakage through preprocessing parameters.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed: (1) Temporal-aware features (treatment group): for each vital and lab, compute (a) the value at the reference time T (most recent observation ≤ T), (b) the rate of change over the prior 6 hours (slope or differenced value), (c) the time since last measurement (in hours), (d) the standard deviation or coefficient of variation of measurements in the prior 6 hours, (e) binary flags for missingness of each signal, and (f) counts of how many measurements were obtained in the prior 6 hours (sampling frequency as a proxy for clinical concern). For labs with batch timing, use the most recent result and time-to-result. Also include static features: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity score (computed from diagnoses up to but not including the 6-hour window), and current medication class (vasopressors, antibiotics prior to sepsis onset). (2) Baseline features (control group): the most recent value of each vital and lab within 24 hours of T, summary statistics over the entire prior 24 hours (min, max, mean of each vital), and the same static features. The treatment features explicitly encode trajectory and recency; the baseline features represent current snapshot + 24-hour summary. Both feature sets exclude any information logged after time T. Feature alignment is ensured by using the same set of vitals and labs in both groups, and the static features are identical.
structured_choices.model: Temporally aware model: a long short-term memory (LSTM) network or temporal convolutional network (TCN) trained on irregularly sampled sequences. Input: for each patient encounter, construct a 12-hour look-back window from T–12h to T, sampled at the actual EHR observation times (irregular intervals). Each time step includes available vitals and labs; missing values are masked (not imputed). The network learns temporal patterns of vital-sign and lab trajectories. Output: a single scalar prediction (probability of sepsis within 6 hours). The model is trained with focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy to handle class imbalance (expected sepsis rate ~5–15%). Hyperparameter tuning (LSTM hidden size, dropout, learning rate, batch size) is performed on the validation set (months 25–30) using random search or Bayesian optimization; the best configuration (as judged by validation AUROC) is then evaluated on the test set.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the static-feature set (most recent values + 24-hour summaries + static covariates) with identical class-weighting and hyperparameter tuning effort. The baseline is fit using scikit-learn's LogisticRegression with L2 regularization; the regularization strength C is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set (months 1–24), using the same metric (AUROC) as the main model. The baseline receives no temporal or trajectory information and represents the standard clinical decision-support paradigm (current status + recent averages). This is a fair comparison because (1) both models are tuned on validation data using the same metric, (2) both handle class imbalance, (3) the baseline is given the richest static and summary features possible without trajectory encoding, and (4) the gap in performance isolates the value of temporal modeling. The baseline is not given the temporal features (e.g., rate of change, sampling frequency) because those are the innovation being tested; if the temporal model underperforms, it would indicate that trajectory information genuinely does not help, not that the model is poorly tuned.
structured_choices.primary_metric: AUROC (area under the receiver-operating characteristic curve) computed on the held-out test set (months 31–36). AUROC is chosen because (1) it is threshold-independent and reflects the model's ability to rank sepsis-positive patients above sepsis-negative patients, which is the core of the discrimination hypothesis, (2) it is robust to class imbalance (unlike accuracy) and does not require choosing an arbitrary decision threshold, and (3) clinically, the goal is to surface high-risk patients for triage; a clinician can then apply their own threshold based on alert fatigue tolerance. The primary hypothesis succeeds if the temporal model's test-set AUROC is statistically significantly higher than the baseline's test-set AUROC (e.g., Δ AUROC > 0.02–0.03 with p < 0.05 via DeLong test or bootstrap).
secondary_metrics: Early-warning recall at fixed false-positive rate (e.g., recall at FPR = 5%, 10%, 20%): reflects the operational goal of catching sepsis cases within the actionable window while controlling alert volume. Computed on the test set.
Sensitivity and specificity at clinically motivated thresholds (e.g., threshold chosen to match existing alert systems' false-positive rate).
Calibration: Brier score and calibration plots (predicted probability vs. observed frequency) on the test set. Miscalibration would suggest the temporal model overfits or drifts in practice.

Stratified performance by site, admission type (ICU vs. ED), and sepsis definition variant (qSOFA alone vs. culture + antibiotics): tests whether gains are consistent across subgroups or driven by label artifacts.
Performance stability: AUROC computed on each month of the test set separately to detect temporal drift within the test period.
Negative control: AUROC of both models when labels are randomly shuffled (should be ~0.5); confirms models are learning signal, not memorizing data.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: The validation set (months 25–30) is used solely for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. For the baseline (logistic regression), C is tuned using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set (months 1–24), and the best C is evaluated on the validation set; the validation AUROC is recorded but not used to further adjust C. For the temporal model (LSTM), hyperparameters are tuned using random search: 50 random configurations of (hidden_size, dropout, learning rate, batch_size) are trained on the training set; each configuration's AUROC on the validation set is recorded; the configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected. Early stopping is applied during training to prevent overfitting: training is halted when validation AUROC plateaus or declines for 10 consecutive epochs. The selected model (both architecture and learned weights) is then frozen and evaluated on the test set exactly once.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36) is locked until the final evaluation and is never used for hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, or model debugging. After model selection is complete (both baseline and temporal model are finalized on validation data), the test set is used exactly once: both models make predictions on all test-set encounters, and primary and secondary metrics are computed. No further model retraining, threshold adjustment, or feature engineering is performed after the test-set evaluation. If additional analyses (e.g., calibration inspection, subgroup breakdowns) are conducted, they are performed post-hoc on the test set with full transparency; no model retuning is done. This one-shot test-set evaluation prevents optimistic bias and ensures the reported metrics reflect true generalization.
structured_choices.confound_controls: 1. **Site-specific documentation and sepsis detection thresholds**: Split analyses by clinical site. If one site has much higher sepsis prevalence or different vital-sign documentation practices, report performance separately for each site and show that gains are not site-dependent. 2. **Label-timing artifacts**: The 6-hour prediction window is defined prospectively from the model's reference time T; no information after T is used. Sensitivity analysis: recompute with 3-hour and 12-hour windows to confirm that the temporal model's gains are not artifacts of window width. 3. **Missing data confounds**: Missingness patterns can correlate with sepsis risk (sicker patients are monitored more). The feature set includes explicit missingness flags and sampling frequency; verify that the temporal model's gain is not purely from learning missingness patterns by training an oracle that sees only missingness indicators and comparing its AUROC to the full model. 4. **Alternate sepsis definitions**: Replicate the analysis using two independent label definitions (e.g., qSOFA ≥ 2 criteria vs. culture + antibiotic rule). If the temporal model outperforms the baseline under both definitions, label artifacts are unlikely. 5. **Baseline feature richness**: Ensure the baseline receives all non-temporal features (static covariates, current values, 24-hour summaries). If the baseline underperforms, it is not due to neglect but to the absence of trajectory information. 6. **Calibration and subgroup fairness**: Examine calibration plots and stratified performance by age, sex, and admission type. Poor calibration or disparity would suggest the temporal model is learning spurious correlations rather than genuine sepsis risk. 7. **Out-of-distribution performance**: If data from a held-out hospital site or time period are available, evaluate both models on this truly external data to confirm generalization.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Vital-Sign Trajectories for Sepsis Prediction

To test the hypothesis that temporally aware models outperform static baselines in 6-hour sepsis prediction, we propose the following rigorous experiment design.

### 1. Data and Labels

We will use retrospective EHR data spanning 36 months from a large hospital system (ICU and ED). The dataset will include all timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, O2 saturation), lab results (lactate, WBC, CRP, blood cultures), medication administrations, and clinical diagnoses. We will include at least 50,000 patient encounters.

Sepsis labels are defined using clinical adjudication: a patient is labeled positive if sepsis onset occurs within 6 hours of a reference observation time T. Onset is marked as the earliest of (a) charted sepsis diagnosis with supporting SIRS or qSOFA criteria, (b) blood culture followed by broad-spectrum antibiotics within a 6-hour window, or (c) clinical review adjudication. Critically, all information used to define the label is timestamped and locked; no information after time T is accessible to the model, and no information after the 6-hour window is backfilled into the label. This prevents leakage and ensures we predict future onset, not already-declared sepsis.

### 2. Temporal Split and Site Stratification

Data are divided chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for held-out testing. This temporal split is essential because (1) it prevents future information from leaking into training, (2) it tests whether the model generalizes under changing clinical practices and sepsis epidemiology, and (3) it mirrors realistic prospective deployment. Within each temporal fold, patient encounters are grouped by clinical site and kept together; no patient appears in multiple splits. This stratification allows us to detect site-specific confounds (e.g., documentation practices, sepsis detection thresholds).

### 3. Feature Engineering: Two Matched Cohorts

**Treatment group (temporal-aware features):** For each vital and lab, we compute (a) the most recent value at time T, (b) the rate of change (slope) over the prior 6 hours, (c) time since last measurement, (d) variability (standard deviation) in the prior 6 hours, (e) a binary flag for missingness, and (f) the frequency of measurements (as a proxy for clinical vigilance). We also include static features: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity score, and current medications. All features use data up to and including time T only.

**Control group (static-baseline features):** The most recent value of each vital and lab (within 24 hours of T), summary statistics over the prior 24 hours (min, max, mean), and the same static covariates. This group has no trajectory or rate-of-change information.

All preprocessing (z-score scaling, categorical encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied uniformly to validation and test sets. Missing values are flagged (not imputed), and the flags themselves become features.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Temporal model:** An LSTM or temporal convolutional network trained on irregularly sampled 12-hour sequences. The input is each vital and lab measurement at its actual EHR timestamp; missing measurements are masked. The network learns temporal patterns and outputs a probability of sepsis within 6 hours. Training uses focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy to handle class imbalance (~5–15% positive rate). Hyperparameters (hidden size, dropout, learning rate, batch size) are tuned via random search (50 configurations) on the training set, and the best configuration (highest validation AUROC) is selected.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on static features and 24-hour summaries. Regularization strength C is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set using AUROC. Both models receive identical class-weighting and tuning effort; the baseline is not deliberately under-tuned.

### 5. Evaluation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 25–30) is used only to select hyperparameters. The test set (months 31–36) is locked and touched exactly once, after all tuning is complete.

**Primary metric:** AUROC on the test set. AUROC is threshold-independent, robust to class imbalance, and clinically meaningful (it ranks sepsis-positive patients higher, allowing clinicians to apply their own alert thresholds). We test the hypothesis with a DeLong test for AUROC difference (α = 0.05); the temporal model succeeds if Δ AUROC ≥ 0.02–0.03.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) Recall at fixed false-positive rates (e.g., 5%, 10%, 20%), reflecting alert volume constraints; (2) calibration (Brier score, calibration plots); (3) stratified performance by site, admission type, and alternate sepsis definitions; (4) temporal stability (AUROC by month within the test set); (5) negative control (AUROC with shuffled labels, should ≈ 0.5).

### 6. Confound Controls

**Label-timing artifacts:** We will recompute performance with 3-hour and 12-hour windows to confirm gains are not artifacts of the chosen window.

**Missingness confounds:** We will train an oracle that sees only missingness indicators and compare its AUROC to the full temporal model. If the full model only marginally exceeds the oracle, gains are driven by missingness patterns, not true trajectories.

**Definition robustness:** We will replicate the analysis under two independent sepsis definitions (qSOFA vs. culture + antibiotic). Consistent gains across definitions rule out label artifacts.

**Calibration and fairness:** We will inspect calibration plots and compute stratified performance by age, sex, and admission type. Poor calibration or subgroup disparity suggests spurious learning.

**Site-specific effects:** We will report performance separately for each site and test for site-model interactions.

This design directly tests whether temporal trajectories improve prediction while controlling for confounds and preventing leakage.
